title: Introduction — purpose and scope of the Manual
text: This Manual of Patent Practice and Procedure has been prepared to serve as a guide for examiners and other officers of the Patent Office in the matter of processing patent applications under the Patents Act, 1970 (as amended). The Manual is also intended for use by applicants, patent agents and attorneys, and members of the public who deal with the Patent Office. It is in no way a substitute for the Act and the Rules made thereunder.

title: Office structure — the Controller and Examiners
text: The Patent Office is headed by the Controller General of Patents, Designs and Trade Marks. The Controller, vested with quasi-judicial functions under the Act, is assisted by Senior Joint Controllers, Joint Controllers, Deputy Controllers and Assistant Controllers, along with Examiners of Patents and Designs. The four branch offices are at Kolkata (head office), Delhi, Mumbai and Chennai.

title: Filing requirements — Form 1 and ancillary forms
text: An application for a patent shall be filed in Form 1 along with the provisional or complete specification (Form 2). The applicant must also file Form 3 for foreign filing information under section 8, Form 5 declaration of inventorship, and where applicable Form 26 power of attorney. The fees prescribed in the First Schedule to the Patent Rules, 2003 apply.

title: First Examination Report (FER) — content and procedure
text: The Examiner shall conduct a substantive examination after the application is referred to him by the Controller under section 12. The Examiner prepares a First Examination Report (FER) recording objections under sections 3, 4, novelty under section 2(1)(l), inventive step under section 2(1)(ja), and any procedural deficiencies. The FER is issued to the applicant under rule 24B(3).

title: FER — substantive objections under section 3 (non-inventions)
text: Section 3 enumerates subject matter that does not qualify as an 'invention' under the Patents Act, 1970. The Examiner must check whether the claimed subject matter falls under any of the bars in section 3(a)-(p). Section 3(d) — the famous anti-evergreening bar on new forms of known substances absent enhanced efficacy — is examined separately. Section 3(k) excludes mathematical or business methods, computer programs per se, and algorithms. Section 3(p) excludes traditional knowledge.

title: FER — examination under section 3(d) (anti-evergreening)
text: When the application claims a new form of a known substance, the Examiner shall require the applicant to demonstrate, by way of comparative data, that the new form results in the enhancement of the known efficacy of the substance. Mere physical-chemical property differences (solubility, stability, hygroscopicity) are not enough; therapeutic efficacy is the touchstone per Novartis AG v Union of India (2013) 6 SCC 1.

title: Response to FER and putting the application in order for grant
text: Under rule 24B(5) the applicant must reply to the FER and put the application in order for grant within six months of issuance of the FER, extendable by three months on request under rule 138. Failure to do so deems the application abandoned under section 21(1).

title: Pre-grant opposition under section 25(1) — examination procedure
text: On receipt of a representation under section 25(1), the Controller shall consider the representation only when a request for examination has been filed. The applicant is given a copy and three months to file a statement and evidence in reply. The opposition is decided by the Controller after hearing both parties.

title: Post-grant opposition under section 25(2) — Opposition Board procedure
text: Within one year of publication of grant of a patent, any person interested may give notice of opposition under section 25(2). The Controller constitutes a three-member Opposition Board under section 25(3), which examines the opposition and submits a recommendation to the Controller. The Controller then hears the parties and decides the opposition.

title: Compulsory licensing under section 84 — grounds and procedure
text: After three years from the date of grant, any person interested may apply for a compulsory licence on the grounds in section 84(1): (a) unmet reasonable public requirements; (b) unaffordability; or (c) failure to work the patented invention in India. The Controller considers the nature of the invention, the applicant's ability to work it, and the patentee's efforts. Natco v Bayer (2012) is the leading domestic precedent — the only compulsory licence granted to date.

title: Form 27 — working statement under section 146(2)
text: Every patentee and licensee must file a statement of working of the patent in Form 27. The Patents (Amendment) Rules, 2024 effective 2024-03-15 changed the cadence from annual to every three financial years and removed the requirement to disclose licensee details. Non-filing attracts a fine up to ten lakh rupees under section 122.

title: PCT national-phase entry in India — timeline and requirements
text: An international application under the Patent Cooperation Treaty designating India may enter the national phase in India within 31 months from the priority date. The applicant files Form 1, Form 2 (national-phase specification) and pays the prescribed fees. Statements under section 8 are required only if there are foreign filings outside the PCT family.
